data.title: SCHD: Time To Throw In The Towel? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Dividend investors once viewed NYSEARCA:SCHD, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™, as the gold standard for income and total returns within the dividend space.

From inception in 2011 until the end of 2022, SCHD kept pace with the market while delivering best-in-class dividend yields and dividend growth, largely off the back of its robust systematic asset selection process. For years, the fund was a clear 'go to' for investors seeking moderate yields, minimal drawdowns, and exposure to high-quality companies in the broader US equity markets.

In 2022, the market regime shifted with the rise of AI and the launch of ChatGPT.

This new regime rewarded growth companies as opposed to dividend payers, and in the last three years, 'Mag 7' stocks seem to have swallowed the market whole. As SCHD has no real exposure to this group, its performance has trailed both the market and investor expectations. This chronic underperformance for the last three years has left many wondering whether or not it’s time to throw in the towel on the ETF.

Our take: while the fund has a strong track record and is optimally positioned for now, in the event of a market selloff, the medium- and long-term future of the fund remains much blurrier.

Today, we’ll take a deeper look at the SCHD's core functionality, examine performance - both pre- and post ChatGPT - and make the case that it's time to stop buying the popular ETF - at least for now.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

The Thesis

We’ve covered SCHD a number of times over the last few years, each time arguing that the fund was worth holding or buying, depending on the market context:

Most recently, we upgraded SCHD to a Strong Buy after the market selloff in April, as historically, the ETF has produced incredibly strong annualized returns after moderate to deep drawdowns.

In previous coverage, we’ve also highlighted the fact that the fund was underperforming, but that it would likely 'mean revert' back to normal following a period of digestion. Unfortunately, that hasn’t happened.

The core thesis with SCHD is that it does two things very well: it gives investors exposure to high-quality companies via its selection methodology, and this stock selection process drives better than average dividend yields and dividend growth while keeping pace with the market.

Construction-wise, the fund invests shareholder capital into 100 dividend paying stocks that are part of the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index.

This index selects stocks via a minimum market cap and liquidity requirement screen, then sorts companies based on a number of yield metrics, including free cash flow to debt, return on equity, yield, and dividend growth rate:

That index, for reference, screens for 10 years of dividend consistency, a market cap of more than $500 million, and minimum liquidity requirements in terms of stock trading volume:

Next, the fund screens for a number of yield metrics:

Then, the top 50% of stocks from this universe (as ranked by Forward Yield) are given a score based on how they do in the following categories: FCF / Debt, ROE, Forward Yield, and the 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate.

From there, the top 100 stocks with the best scores are selected for the [index], subject to a few diversification requirements.

Generally, we're quite happy with this approach, as investor capital ends up flowing into stable companies that pay a relatively competitive, consistent, well-backed yield.

Historically, this construction paradigm has produced strong performance, strong bounce back potential from crashes, and excellent dividend growth, metrics.

Between SCHD's inception and ChatGPT’s launch in November 2022, the fund produced total returns of 331%, while the S&P 500 produced total returns of 304%:

At the same time, SCHD also managed to grow annual dividend payouts by roughly 11% per year, with significant growth coming between 2019 and 2022:

Whether you were invested in SCHD through this period for total returns or income, the ETF delivered. Entering 2023, the thesis was simple: SCHD's successful asset selection mechanism would likely continue to produce robust returns and income.

Performance

However, as we look now, we can see that the fund's performance can really be broken down into two time periods: pre-and post ChatGPT launch.

Pre-ChatGPT, SCHD was a top fund earning solid marks for strong returns and diversification. As we just laid out, performance was great pre-ChatGPT, and SCHD managed to grow income quite a bit over the same span. As far as income investments go, SCHD was about as good as you could get over this period of time.

That said, since the launch of ChatGPT, the fund has significantly underperformed the market, with SPY returning roughly 69% since November 2022, while SCHD returned a mere 15%:

This performance divergence has left many investors scratching their heads, wondering what went wrong.

In our view, this performance difference is mostly down to the Mag 7 stocks and the fact that the S&P 500 is exposed to these companies, while SCHD is not. Over the last few years, these companies have come to represent a massive part of the market, something we’ve written about here and here.

Make no mistake, we don’t feel as though this is a good thing, and so in some ways, SCHD is underexposed to significant concentration and valuation risk that normal index investors have. In the short term, we’d argue that this is actually a very good thing for SCHD, and so we’re anticipating a period of outperformance over the next few quarters for the more conservative fund.

That said, there’s no guarantee that the 'Mag 7' stocks will mean revert lower, and over the long-term they could continue to dominate. This recent earnings season showed that even as these companies are rewarded rich multiples by the market, their earnings growth and scale - to some degree - justify the premium.

It’s also worth noting that in the last five years, SCHD has begun to develop a pattern of performance where it experiences drawdowns just as significant as the regular index, while bouncing back less convincingly. Going back to the start of December 2024 through April lows, we can see that with SCHD, you're down essentially the same amount as the broader market, but your position has not bounced back nearly as much in the months following:

Similarly, when markets corrected in mid 2023, between the summer and the end of October, SCHD mirrored the S&P 500’s drawdown, but once again delivered subpar post crash returns:

It’s a complicated picture right now for SCHD investors to know whether or not to stick with the fund or call it quits.

The Verdict

As we see it, there’s no definitive way to know whether SCHD's underperformance is permanent or cyclical.

For those who feel that it is cyclical, we understand this position in the sense that there’s a stronger track record for the fund in a pre-AI world than there is in a post AI world. As AI investments mature and begin paying dividends, they will likely be subsumed into SCHD’s overall strategy, which will likely erase any meaningful performance differential.

At the same time, we also see the argument that as AI productivity gains filter through to the rest of the market, SCHD will structurally position investors in the weakest growth companies that are returning capital to shareholders instead of investing in internal projects.

While this argument could be made at any point over the last 13 years, with the advent of AI, we feel as though it could be a very long time until current market dynamics are digested fully enough to impact SCHD positively.

Taken together, we feel as though downgrading the fund to a 'Hold' for now remains the best course of action. Given our recent Sell rating on the market, SCHD appears more well positioned with respect to the concentration and valuation risks we're worried about.

That said, over the medium and longer term, we also feel as though SCHD will likely continue to sub-optimally position investors towards the best total return opportunities, which could have far-reaching implications for your money

As a result, we’re downgrading SCHD for now until such time as we feel it’s prudent to re-upgrade - or even downgrade - the fund.

We’re not allocating any fresh capital to SCHD for the time being, either.

Cheers

data.title: Tariffs Could Be A Boon For Ethan Allen Interiors
data.description:
One company that I have been generally bullish about over time is none other than Ethan Allen Interiors (NYSE:ETD). This is a firm that produces and sells home furnishings and accents, casegoods, and the like. To be honest with you, this is not an industry that I typically would find attractive. And that's because of low margins, competitive pressures, and the likelihood that sales growth will not be significant over the long haul. But when the stock is cheap enough, I believe that an optimistic view is appropriate.

So far, my call on the business has proven to be successful. Since I last reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ candidate back in May of this year, the stock is up 12.3%, essentially matching the 12.2% rise that the S&P 500 has seen over the same window of time. But since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in November of 2021, the stock has jumped 47.9%. That's enough above the 41.3% increase that the broader market experienced to call this a successful play. Interestingly, the stock rose about 2.1% after news broke that the US government plans to impose 30% tariffs on furniture imports. Historically, a large portion of furniture sold in the US is imported. And it is true that Ethan Allen Interiors imports a good chunk of what it sells from other places like China and Taiwan. But most of the goods that it pushes out for this market come from North America.

Even though I expect the US economy to enter into a recession soon, I do believe that this could prove bullish for the company in the near term. It will certainly face some pressure from tariffs, even if indirectly, because of the fact that many of the items that are put into their goods still come from overseas. But considering how cheap the company is and the fact that it has a hefty net cash position of $136.1 million compared to its market capitalization of $728.7 million, I believe that it justifies a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

A look at the bullish case

Perhaps the best place to start with this particular call would be the most recent data provided by management. This data covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. To be clear, 2025 was not particularly great for the business. Revenue in the final quarter came in at $160.4 million. That represents a decline of 4.9% compared to the $168.6 million the business reported one year earlier. Management blamed this sales decline on low consumer confidence, challenging housing market conditions, and trade policies. In the chart below, you can see just how low consumer confidence is compared to what it had been over the last several years. And as I have detailed in other articles, like here and here, there are significant challenges facing the housing market and risks because of the tariff situation.

Regarding housing, the fact of the matter is that when home buying activity is weak, there is less incentive for individuals to invest in furniture. This is because individuals often will change up their furniture when they move to a new home. In addition to this, when home buying conditions are problematic for consumers, it likely means that budgets are constrained, which should in theory lead to lower purchases in the furniture market. Interestingly, however, on the retail side of things, which is where Ethan Allen Interiors gets most of its revenue, industry conditions are actually pretty solid.

In the chart above, you can see monthly retail furniture sales according to the Federal Reserve. And in the chart below, you can see the year over year change as time has progressed. All of this data covers the trailing 12 months. During that time, retail furniture sales came in at $137 billion. That's up 4.6% compared to the $131 billion that the industry reported the previous year. In some months, such as December of last year, we have seen year over year spikes as high as 8.4%. And we have not seen a single down month since September of 2024.

This data suggests, at least to me, that there might be some competitive pressures going on here. It's also likely that recent gains have been pumped up by the pass-through costs of tariffs. Regardless, it is clear that while the industry is experiencing revenue growth more broadly, Ethan Allen Interiors has endured weakness. This is disappointing, but it's not the end of the world, as you will soon see.

As revenue for the company declined, profitability decreased also. Net income contracted from $18.5 million to $12.3 million. But on an adjusted basis, the decline was from $18.1 million to $12.5 million. Other profitability metrics for the business worsened without exception. Operating cash flow, for instance, dipped from $26.2 million to $24.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $30.1 million to $27.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business dropped from $26 million to $19.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. For this time, we actually have more data because of how year-end reporting works. The drop in revenue from $646.2 million to $614.6 million is undeniably painful in and of itself. In addition to seeing retail net sales drop 3.2%, wholesale net sales declined by 3.2% as well. According to management, the design centers that the company has saw lower traffic and fewer contract sales that more than offset higher average ticket prices.

The company as a whole delivered lower volumes in light of broader economic weakness. Some of the pain was certainly the result of lower shipments to the government, with those revenues plunging 23.7% because of a slowdown in government spending. And as revenue dropped, profits and every cash flow metric that I looked at declined. Considering that this is a low margin industry to begin with, with high fixed costs, profit declines of this magnitude are not shocking. And that's because fixed costs have to be spread across lower revenue dollars coming in.

If we use the results that we have seen for the 2024 and 2025 fiscal years, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Although the price-to-earnings multiple fits more in the fair value range as I see it, the other two trading multiples put it in the undervalued range. Even compared to some other similar firms, I would argue that the business is on the cheaper end of the spectrum. In the table below, I stacked it up against five other enterprises of a similar nature. On a price-to-earnings basis, two of the five firms were cheaper than it is. And when it came to the other two approaches, one was cheaper while another was tied with it.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Ethan Allen Interiors 13.9 7.1 7.6
The Lovesac Company (LOVE) 27.5 25.6 12.8
Flexsteel Industries (FLXS) 13.4 7.1 7.6
Bassett Furniture Industries (BSET) 54.3 10.7 15.4
Leggett & Platt (LEG) 8.5 3.9 7.0
Hooker Furnishings Corporation (HOFT) 129.5 19.0 21.2

On September 26th, some interesting political news shook up this industry. Donald Trump announced, effective October 1st, the US government will be imposing a 30% tariff on all finished upholstered furniture imports. There will be an even larger 50% tariff on kitchen and bathroom cabinetry and vanities. Normally, you would expect this to prove problematic. After all, according to one source, 60% of all furniture sold in the US it was imported from Asia alone. This includes 86% of wood furniture and 42% of upholstered furniture.

However, relative to other similar firms, Ethan Allen Interiors might be a beneficiary of this maneuver. According to management, about 75% of the furniture that it sells is manufactured in its North American plants. This includes plants in Vermont and North Carolina, but also in Mexico and Honduras. The rest comes from other countries like Vietnam and Indonesia. Of course, the company sells other goods as well. But a full 80% of all goods that it sells come from North America. Unfortunately, we don't know how much of the production comes specifically from the US market compared to Mexico and Honduras. But the fact that the company has a sizable physical presence here could work to its advantage, especially if it is able to play around with manufacturing capacity here at home.

This is great in and of itself, but I am still worried about this industry more broadly. As I stated already, I firmly believe that the US economy is heading toward a recession. And if that does come to fruition, you can expect furniture sales to weaken industry-wide. Having said that, the company is well positioned to survive in most any environment. It has no debt on its books and enjoys $136.1 million in cash and cash equivalents. For a firm with a market capitalization of only $728.7 million, that is a massive amount of protection.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I continue to believe that Ethan Allen Interiors makes for a decent prospect. The firm is attractively priced on both an absolute basis and relative to some other similar firms. Although it has experienced weakness as of late, this tariff announcement could work in the firm's favor. When you add all of this together, I think that maintaining it as a soft ‘buy’ candidate is the appropriate move.

data.title: Logan Energy: Big Production Increases Ahead After A Big Second Quarter
data.description:
Logan Energy (OTCPK:LOECF) reported a big second quarter where the company broke firmly into the black and operating costs came way down as the company continues to gain economies of scale (and no pipes broke in the current fiscal year). The last article mentioned how there were a lot of startup and other nonrecurring costs still coming through after all the acquisitions reported in the previous fiscal year (combined with cash raises). This company is attempting to gain a minimum size of operations quickly to minimize some of the small company risks (one of which was shown by what that broken pipe did to operating costs in previous articles in the first fiscal year).

Earnings

There is going to be some easy cost comparisons as a lot of one-time items fade because the size now makes those items insignificant to results. For many companies, 10,000 BOED is an important goal for startups. This company went past that goal and is clearly going to get to 20,000 BOED in less than a year. Note that this company is a Canadian company reporting in Canadian dollars.

One of the things about the acquired acreage is that much of the acreage is like the Permian in the United States in that it already has existing production. But in many cases, that existing production is dry gas production (from way back when). Additionally, operating costs are coming in line because there is no broken pipe to fix on a very low startup volume.

But the new technology is allowing the company to find liquids on this same acreage (along with natural gas, of course). That is slowly changing the production mixture of the acquired acreage as new wells are drilled. That is the reason important products like oil are climbing much faster than natural gas to create a more valuable production mix.

Since this is essentially a startup operation that began with an extremely low number for production figures after the spinoff, it is no surprise that the company is reporting a minimal disturbance to the average selling price while the whole industry has considerably more significantly lower per barrel revenue.

It is also important to note that management spent roughly 80% of the budget in the first half. Now comes the cash flow with not a whole lot more budgeted expenditures. That cash burn shown above is about to reverse really fast.

Debt did climb. But a lot of the initial construction is now over.

As can be seen above, selling prices did decline. But that was largely offset by the increase in a more valuable production mix as new wells came online. The net result of all of this is a far more valuable operating netback, which helped earnings to cross firmly into the black and cash flow to grow tremendously, as management promised.

As the last several articles have noted, this management has built and sold companies before. So, it can easily handle the growth you see above that might stymie other managements. Budgeting cash adequately is also not likely to be an issue when management has this kind of experience.

Pouce Coup

What will happen is the production comes online to produce cash flow and actually reduce that debt balance by the end of the fiscal year. Here is the main basis for that assertion.

This was always meant to be a "slam dunk" in the budget goals. The money was there to build and start up the necessary plant and infrastructure. Now the wells are being hooked up and coming online. The first production really began in June, so the production figure shown above is at best for 1 month of the quarter.

But this management has a goal to fill up that plant to capacity with wells already drilled. That means investors could see up to 10,000 BOED production from this asset in the third and fourth quarters.

This is meant to be a free cash flow asset that will fund other projects. However, as North America gains the ability to export natural gas and data centers proliferate, gas prices may justify an increase in production in this particular area in the future. It is an option available if and when the time comes.

This particular project constituted the majority of the production increase for the fiscal year. Really, the hard part (and the risk) is largely completed. Now it's "just" producing from here on out.

Budget Progress

The budget remains largely intact. There were always going to be a lot of projects in the first half that produced cash flow in the second half. Most of those projects are shown in the key area, Pouce Coupe. That acreage alone has 9 new wells coming into production and a now operating plant to process the production. It was a big chunk of the budget.

Even though cash flow increased quite a bit in the second quarter, it will probably continue to climb in the third and fourth quarters because the production increases are relatively large compared to the existing production volume.

That means that the projected cash flow available to repay some debt to end with the budgeted debt shown above is very reasonable (as of right now).

The only thing about all this new production is that the average decline rate next year will likely be higher than many investors are seeing elsewhere because most of the production is first year production. It will likely take a few years to establish an average production decline rate that is comparable to much of the industry. Remember that the first-year production decline of either conventional or unconventional is relatively steep (with unconventional production declines being much higher in the first year).

Summary

Logan Energy now has production in excess of the important 10,000 BOED and is well on its way to 20,000 BOED. There should be some operational savings with that level of production. As production climbs, management may gain some bargaining power over prices received for production or find that the production can be moved to stronger markets (in the case of natural gas).

Cash flow was largely negative in the first half of the fiscal year as management built an oil and gas processing plant while drilling a fair number of wells. That plant is now operating, and wells are steadily coming online.

Now cash flow is going to switch from negative to positive, which should enable the current debt load to decline to budgeted levels for the yearend.

From hereon in, the company is going to have to "live within its means" unless they decide that a deal comes along where more stock is used. That happened a few times in the last fiscal year. So far, this year seems to be more about getting operations going.

Earnings now seem to be firmly in the black unless there is a recession or worse to severely weaken commodity prices. In any event, the financial situation of the company appears to be strong so that the company can handle a cyclical downturn if it happens.

This management has built and sold companies before. I would therefore keep this idea as a strong buy with the idea of potentially holding it until management sells the company.

Risks

The current political uncertainty appears to be causing a recession in the future. If the current threat of trade wars and tariffs is not replaced by something constructive and permanent, it may cause something worse. That could be very bad news for the industry, let alone this company.

There is a lot of brand-new acreage that holds a lot of promise. Any or all of the acreage could fail to meet the expectations of management.

The loss of services of key senior management could prove to be very difficult to replace. Managements that build and sell companies are relatively rare in any industry. Therefore, replacing key upper managers for any reason could prove to be a challenge. This situation could unfavorably impact the future of the company or even cause a premature sale.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SoFi Technologies: Ride Your Winners - Fed Inflection Fully Priced In (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
SOFI's Rally Occurred Overly Fast & Furious, Warranting A Downgrade To Hold

We previously covered SoFi Technologies (NASDAQ:SOFI) in May 2025, discussing how the prior selloff had already triggered the much-needed correction, thanks to the cheaper valuations and the more than doubling upside potential over the next few years.

Combined with the ongoing diversification in revenues across net interest incomes/fee-based platforms, the increased adoption of its well-diversified financial products to its existing/new members as a one-stop shop fintech platform, and the outsized lending/neobank performance, we had believed that the stock was likely to outperform moving forward.

SOFI 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating, SOFI has delivered a rich +121.6% rally compared to the wider market at +16.9%, while well outperforming most of its fintech peers by large degrees.

Much of its tailwinds are naturally attributed to the reduced risks of a potential recession in the near term, thanks to the normalizing yield curve in the short- and long-term interest rates at 0.44% by September 2025.

This is significantly aided by the Fed's recent pivot in the September 2025 FOMC meeting, as they hint at further rate cuts before the end of 2025 - with it signaling the much needed normalization in the macroeconomic environment after the challenging past few years.

These developments paint a highly optimistic macro picture for the fintech sector indeed, since they are highly sensitive to the fluctuating interest rates and, consequently, the consumer lending trends, with it naturally explaining their recent stock price outperformance.

SOFI has further fed the rally with a twice raised FY2025 guidance, with the net revenues of $3.375B (+26.2% YoY), adj EBITDA of $960M (+44.1% YoY), and adj EPS at $0.31 (+106.6% YoY) building upon the robust FY2024 outperformance at +25.9% YoY, +54.5% YoY, and +250% YoY, respectively.

These numbers do not appear to be overly aggressive as well, thanks to the H1 '25 outperformance at +30.6% YoY, +62.7% YoY, and +366.6% YoY, respectively, with it strongly hinting at another beat and raise performance in the upcoming FQ3 '25 earnings call sometime in November 2025.

Much of SOFI's tailwinds are attributed to the management's ability to drive a diversified "business towards more capital light fee-based revenue streams" as FQ2 '25 brought forth an increasingly well balanced revenue mix from the Lending segment to Financial Services/Technology Platform segment at a ratio of 48.6% (-6.9 points YoY)/51.3% (+6.9 points YoY), respectively.

The increased top-line contribution from the Financial Services/Technology Platform segment naturally solidifies its diversified fintech investment thesis against that of a glorified neo-bank - a strategy that is likely to trigger its more resilient operations while moderating its future sensitivity to the fluctuating interest rates.

SOFI's Robust Lending Prospects

Otherwise, thanks to the likely to be cheaper borrowing costs, we believe that SOFI's Lending segment is likely to outperform from Q4 '25 onwards, attributed to the higher origination and/or refinancing of its personal/student/home loan offerings.

This may be built upon the robust loan origination growth observed in FQ2 '25, significantly aided by the relatively reasonable credit performance thanks to their strategic focus on borrowers with higher FICO scores.

At the same time, we have observed a growing adoption of SOFI's one stop shop fintech platform, as observed in the higher net new member growth at 846K in FQ2 '25 (+5.4% QoQ/+31.5% YoY) and new product adoption growth at 1.26M (+6.7% QoQ/+34% YoY) - with it exemplifying how the improved cross-selling trends have triggered the accelerating fintech growth as discussed above.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus has further raised their forward estimates, with SOFI expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +24.7%/+68.3% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +12.7%/+62.1% a year ago, while building the 3Y historical top-line growth at a CAGR of +37.1%, with it exemplifying the fintech's accelerating growth profile thanks to its robust member acquisition trends and the improving operational scale across its neobank/fintech segments.

SOFI Valuations

Despite this, we are uncertain if it is wise for the market to upgrade SOFI's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 91.49x, with it implying a drastic premium compared to our last Buy rating levels at 46.56x, the 1Y mean of 58.09x, the sector median of 11.28x, and its direct fintech peers at:

  • Affirm (AFRM) at 33.07x,
  • PayPal (PYPL) at 13x,
  • Upstart (UPST) at 36.69x, and
  • Block (XYZ) at 29.87x.

This comparison has naturally highlighted why SOFI's rally of +196% from the April 2025 bottom has occurred overly fast and furiously, with a similar premium also observed in its elevated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.33x, compared to:

  • AFRM at 0.92x,
  • PYPL at 1.07x, and
  • UPST at 0.92x,
  • Aside from XYZ at 2.64x,

with it apparent that a moderate pullback to our Buy zones may be necessary for an improved return profile, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is SOFI Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SOFI 5Y Stock Price

For now, SOFI has had an impressive upward rally since the Q3 '24 floor and the April 2025 bottom, while breaking out a new peak of $30s beyond the pandemic heights of $25s after running away from its 50/100/200 day moving averages.

SOFI's Technical Indicators

Despite this, we are already seeing hints of peaking in SOFI's trading volumes, with historical trends implying a deep pullback in the near-term after the bulls' buying power is exhausted.

This conjecture is not overly bearish as well, since the stock is already trading at a notable premium to our fair value estimates of $14.40, based on the management's raised FY2025 adj. EPS guidance at $0.31 (+106.6% YoY) and our last Buy rating P/E levels at 46.56x (nearer to the peer group mean of 40.82x).

The same may be observed against its Tangible Book Value per share of $4.44, based on the FQ2 '25 Tangible Book Value of $5.25B and the last share count of 1.18B, with it apparent that the recent optimism surrounding the Fed rate cut and the fintech's robust performance metrics has been overly done.

SOFI has pulled forward much of its upside potential to our long-term price target of $33.50 as well, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $0.72, with it offering interested investors a minimal margin of safety.

While there appears to be an excellent more than doubling upside potential to our bull-case long-term price target of $65.80, it is highly contingent on the stock being able to sustain its premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 91.49x - a rather speculative notion, since it relies on the fintech delivering near triple-digit adj. EPS growth over the next few years of macroeconomic normalization.

Pending further clarity on its H2 '25/FY2026 performance, we do not recommend anyone to add at current heights, attributed to the potentially painful capital losses once the market exuberance is moderated and the stock pulls back.

While long-term investors may want to celebrate their wins, we prefer to downgrade the SOFI stock to a Hold here instead.

Interested investors may want to observe the stock price movement for a little longer before adding, preferably upon a deep retracement to the 200-day moving averages of $16s, with those levels nearer to our fair value estimates of $14s.

Given the potential -46% downside at the midpoint from current levels, some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Gilat Satellite Networks: Strong Top-Line Growth And Order Build Makes This Stock A Buy
data.description:
Shares of Gilat Satellite Networks Ltd. (NASDAQ:GILT), a global provider of satellite network solutions, has surged 140.25% year-over-year (YoY). The stock is on a solid momentum and is currently trading just 6% below its 52-week high of $12.45. I believe we are yet to see the best of this company, based on its recent record earnings. Quarterly revenues jumped 37.08% (YoY) even as it raised its annual EBITDA guidance range to $50 million-$53 million from a low of $47 million set in Q1 2025. I will explain why this company is a buy based on growing contract volumes, higher top-line growth, and increasing M&A activity into 2026.

Robust Pipeline And Order Growth Into 2026

Firstly, Gilat has seen phenomenal growth since it finalized its acquisition of US firm, DataPath, Inc., back in November 2023. I would describe this purchase as a strategic gateway for Gilat’s entry into the US defense market. The satellite network technology company recently announced that Gilat DataPath had been awarded a $7+ million contract by the US Department of Defense for its proprietary “DKET 3421 transportable SATCOM terminals.” While the company expects to fulfill this delivery by the end of 2025, we should focus on the backlog build at the hands of the company into H2 2025. This $7 million contract represents about 3.7% of Gilat’s pending orders secured and announced in its Q2 2025 SEC Filings.

As of Q2 2025, Gilat had at least $189 million worth of orders, including a $60 million order in Peru to offer Digital Inclusion Solutions. By August 2025, Gilat announced that it had signed an additional contract from Peru, worth $25 million, which I believe translates to $85 million, bringing the order build above $210 million. Take note that this order value does not include the backlog on Gilat’s pending list. It is worth noting that Gilat’s Skyedge IV platform increased its order book value to $40 million in Q2 2025 from $15 million in Q1 2025 (an increase of 166.67%, QoQ). Then again, Gilat acquired Stella Blu Solutions in January 2025, and by Q2 2025, it had already secured a $27 million contract.

Having said this, the company is aggressively expanding its tentacles from Israel to the rest of the world in offering mission-critical communication systems. Even better, the ratification of new orders into H2 2025 informed the company’s decision to raise the midpoint of both its revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance for FY 2025. For annual revenue, the previous guidance was the range of $415 million to $455 million (a midpoint of $435 million), while adjusted EBITDA was set at a range of $47 million to $53 million (a midpoint of $50 million). Into H2 2025, Gilat revised the earnings guidance upwards, with revenues at $435 million to $455 million (a midpoint of $445 million, an increase of 45.71%, YoY, from $305.4 million realized in FY 2024). For adjusted EBITDA, it was revised to the range of $50 million to $53 million (a midpoint of $51.5 million, representing an increase of 22.04%, YoY- from $42.2 million recorded in FY 2024).

As of 2025, the global defense market is forecast to reach $56.4 billion and is expected to be valued at $145 billion by 2035 (growing at a CAGR of 9.9% over the forecast period).

Valuation

I will use the EV/EBITDA multiple to value Gilat, as it will give an expanded view of its operational performance (with the understanding that it expects higher earnings into 2026). It will also encompass the value of the company, including the M&A activities (as elucidated in this article).

Gilat’s forward EV/EBITDA ratio stands at 14.78X. As explained earlier, Gilat’s revised its adjusted EBITDA guidance to a midpoint of $51.5 million. I will multiply the forward ratio of 14.78 by the forward guidance of $51.5 million to get the forward EV, which is $761.17 million. To get the market capitalization, I will subtract the debt of $66.02 million from the enterprise value and add its cash balance of $64.93 million, giving us $760.08 million. At this point, we will divide it by the total outstanding share count to get the future price.

It is key to mention that Gilat announced a private placement of about 7 million shares that will see the company receive about $65 million as net proceeds (while selling the stock at $9.35 per share). One thing to mention here is that this placement will only “be made in Israel” and is not expected to affect US shareholders. The exclusion of these shares from the US SEC filing means that we will use the total outstanding share count of 57,189,406 shares as previously recorded in Q2 2025. Gilat’s future price thus stands at $13.30, representing an increase of 12.03% (YoY), showing that the stock is slightly undervalued.

I will compare Gilat’s performance with some of its peers under the same metric.

Forward EV/EBITDA Fwd. EBITDA Fwd. EV Fwd. Market Cap Share Count (OUT) Future Price Current Price Valuation
GILT 14.78X $51.5 million $761.17 million $760.08 million $57.2 million $13.30 $11.70 +12% (YoY)
Ituran Location and Control (ITRN) 6.68X $100 million $668 million $751.56 million 19.9 million $37.77 $34.70 +8.13% (YoY)
Ribbon Communications (RBBN) 7.97X $135 million $1.076 billion $727.15 million 177.1 million $4.11 $3.82 +7% (YoY)

This analysis shows that GILT, ITRN, and RBBN are all undervalued, with GILT showing the highest possible upsurge of 12%. Over the past year, all these stocks have outperformed the S&P500 index, which recorded a 15% (YoY) growth.

However, among the 3 companies, only ITRN is offering a dividend, with an annual yield of 5.76% and a forward annual rate of $2. The 4-year average of ITRN’s yield sits at 3.31%, which is about 117% higher than the sector’s median yield of 1.52%.

Risk

Gilat realized a 30.74% (YoY) decline in its cash balance to $64.9 million from a high of $93.7 million as of Q2 2024. While revenues reach record levels in Q2 2025, a decline in earnings into 2026 or failure to meet its guidance will affect the company’s valuation, as elucidated in this article.

Further, untimely or late delivery and fulfillment of the stated orders will also affect Gilat’s expected income by the end of 2025. For instance, the company stated that it expected to fulfill the DataPath order before the end of 2025, and a breach of this contract from Gilat’s side will adversely affect revenue attainment into 2026.

Bottom Line

I have rated Gilat Satellite Networks as a buy due to its growing contract volumes, higher top-line momentum, and increasing strategic M&A activity into 2026. Gilat has even secured additional contracts, as seen in Peru, into H2 2025 already, with a backlog from H1 2025. My analysis has also shown that Gilat is slightly undervalued, as are some of its peers, and is already trading close to its 52-week high.

data.title: MV Oil Trust: High Yield, But Closing Up Shop In 2026
data.description:
If you're looking for a different approach for high-yield investing in the Energy sector, you may want to research Royalty Trusts.

Royalty trusts are mostly within the Exploration & Production, "E&P", Energy industry - these entities earn passive income from arrangements with E&P companies that handle the operations on the Trust's property, and pay the Trust a percentage of the income. MV Oil Trust (NYSE:MVO) is virtually a pure play on crude oil, with ~99% of its income coming from crude. The revenues from this oil production are typically received by MV Partners one month after production. Our previous article about MVO advised "moving on to other high-yield investments rather than holding MVO."

Why? Because MVO is due to terminate in July 2026, after which it will cease paying distributions:

"The Trustee will make a final quarterly cash distribution, if any, on or about July 25, 2026 to the Trust unitholders of record on the 15th day following June 30, 2026, and the Trust Units are expected to be cancelled shortly thereafter. The Trust will not be entitled to any net proceeds that MV Partners receives after the Termination Date from the sale of production from the underlying properties.

The Trust will dissolve and commence winding up its business and affairs after the Termination Date, and, once the Trust winds up and terminates, it will pay no further distributions." (MVO Q2 '25 10Q)

Company Profile

MVO's earnings come from a profits interest of 80% of the net proceeds attributable to the sale of production from the underlying properties during the term of the Trust.

These net profits interests will terminate on the later to occur of (1) June 30, 2026 or (2) the time when 14.4 million barrels of oil equivalent ("MMBoe") have been produced from the underlying properties and sold (which amount is the equivalent of 11.5 MMBoe with respect to the Trust's net profits interest), and the Trust will soon thereafter wind up its affairs and terminate.

"As of June 30, 2025, cumulatively, since inception, the Trust has received payment for 80% of the net proceeds attributable to MV Partners' interest from the sale of 15.0 million barrels of oil equivalent ("MMBoe") of production from the underlying properties (which amount is the equivalent of 12.0MMBoe with respect to the Trust's net profits interest)." (MVO Q2 '25 10Q)

Earnings

While 2024's earnings were stable vs. 2023's, Q1-2 '25 has been a radically different story, with NPI down 33.5%, and Distributable Income down 35%.

Dividends

MVO had a strong 5-year dividend growth rate of 28.77%, with payouts on the rise from late 2020 into late 2022. However, since Q1 '24, when MVO paid $.465, it's been a trip mostly back down the mountain, to the most recent amount of $.185, its lowest since Q1 2021.

At its 9/25/25 closing price of $6.07, MVO yielded ~17%, based upon its trailing quarterly distributions.

MVO's corpus, i.e., capital or principal amount, declined from $3.865M at 12/31/24, to $2.94M as of 6/30/25. It was ~$6M as of 9/30/23:

Performance

While MVO's price performance lagged a bit vs. its industry's average 142% gain over the past 5 years, it's still up 128%, vs. 99% for the S&P 500. The price gain winner over the past 5 years is the broad Energy industry, which is up 171%.

However, income investors didn't buy MVO for potential price gains - it was all about those quarterly distributions, which were over $6.00/unit over the past 5 years. Investors who bought MVO at its depressed September '20 price of ~$2.68/unit or less have received over 2X their investment in the past 5 years.

With the trust ready to terminate in less than a year, it's easy to see why it has had negative price performance, down 34% in the past year, and down 24.4% so far in 2025.

Risks

Net Loss of Principal: Most likely, MVO will keep drifting down in price in the remaining ~10 months until its termination date, resulting in a negative total return.

Parting Thoughts

We advise selling MVO - it's unlikely that it'll pay out anywhere near as much as its current cost of ~$6.00 before it terminates.

If you're interested in other high-yield vehicles, we cover them every Friday and Sunday in our articles.

All tables are furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Regeneron: Strong R&D Pipeline Drives Growth Outlook
data.description:
About two and a half months have passed since my last article, "Regeneron Pharmaceuticals: My Highest Conviction Pharmaceutical Pick for 2025," and during that time, its stock price has risen by 2.5%.

In it, I presented an analysis of clinical data on Dupixent for the treatment of CRSwNP, the prospects for Lynozyfic in the R/R multiple myeloma treatment market following its FDA approval on July 2, and explained why I believe the pressure from biosimilars to Eylea on Regeneron Pharmaceuticals' EBIT (NASDAQ:REGN) will be temporary.

If I look at Regeneron from a technical analysis perspective, its stock price has once again reached a strong resistance zone [$598-$605], but this time, trading volumes were higher than on August 22.

Partly because the EMA's CHMP recommended approval of Dupixent for the treatment of the inflammatory skin condition called chronic spontaneous urticaria [CSU], in my opinion, this event played a key role in weakening the "bears" and will help Regeneron finally begin a new 'bull run' in early October.

However, before I move on to discussing its financial results for Q2 2025, I have a question.

What are Regeneron Pharmaceuticals' recent R&D achievements?

Regeneron is starting to explore the obesity market

So, one of the latest milestones, which, to my great surprise, Mr. Market did not pay attention to, was the publication of the Phase 2 COURAGE study on September 17.

It evaluates the feasibility of adding garetosmab and trevogrumab to Novo Nordisk's Wegovy [2.4 mg semaglutide] (NVO), which, as you know, is, along with Eli Lilly's Zepbound (LLY), the most popular treatment option for patients with obesity.

Patients taking the three-drug combination [trevogrumab 400 mg+garetosmab 10 mg/kg+semaglutide 2.4 mg] lost only 0.9 kg of lean mass, compared to 3.3 kg in those taking Wegovy.

Also, I was surprised to discover that this "triplet" also significantly enhanced fat loss.

So, by 26 weeks from the start of treatment, it led to a reduction in fat mass in 147 patients by an average of 11.8 kg [P<0.001], compared with 7.6 kg for those taking trevogrumab 200 mg+semaglutide 2.4 mg and compared with 6.7 kg for those taking semaglutide alone.

Let's continue.

Not only the "bears" but I too was shocked by the strong performance of Libtayo and Dupixent

Regeneron Pharmaceuticals' revenue was about $3.68 billion in Q2 2025, beating the consensus estimate by about $395 million and my expectation by $125 million.

Meanwhile, it increased by 3.7% year-on-year and 21.5% quarter-on-quarter despite the continued muted demand for Praluent [PCSK9 inhibitor], which is being developed jointly with Sanofi (SNY) to reduce cholesterol and the risk of heart problems.

The second reason that put pressure on Regeneron's revenue was the expected decline in Eylea sales due to the factors I already mentioned in the article "Why Regeneron's Price May Soon Break Out".

But looking a little deeper, despite tougher competition from Roche Holding's Vabysmo (OTCQX:RHHBY) and Amgen's Pavblu (AMGN), its sales reached $754 million, thereby increasing by 2.4% quarter-on-quarter.

On the other hand, Regeneron's non-GAAP EPS blew my mind by reaching $12.89 for the three months ended June 30, 2025, beating my expectations by $1.20 and, more importantly, up 11.5% year over year.

Why did I expect a more modest result?

Regeneron has an extremely rich pipeline of 45 product candidates, 13 of which are in Phase III studies.

In part because it is accelerating investment in mid/late-stage clinical development programs for fianlimab [oncology], linvoseltamab [hematology], and garetosmab [rare disease], this is certainly reflected in the continued growth of its R&D expenses, which amounted to $1.28 billion in Q2 2025.

One of the interesting things I noticed during the quarterly earnings call was the comments from Leonard Schleifer, who is the CEO, about where Regeneron Pharmaceuticals' R&D is heading.

We continue to make significant investments in R&D that have yielded notable progress across several key programs so far this year, which George will discuss in just a moment.

Over the next 6 months, we anticipate Phase III data for our C5 program in generalized myasthenia gravis. For fianlimab, our LAG-3 antibody in combination with Libtayo in advanced melanoma. For garetosmab, our Activin-A antibody in fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva or FOP, and our programs for birch and cat allergies.

We also expect to make a decision on next steps for itepekimab in COPD. Several differentiated early clinical and preclinical programs spanning hematology, genetic medicines, ophthalmology, oncology, and immunology represent an exciting next wave of innovations at Regeneron.

And he also said.

We are investing over $7 billion in the U.S. over the coming years to expand our research and development capabilities and our manufacturing network including a brand-new state-of-the-art fill/finish manufacturing facility in Rensselaer, New York.

Then I ask the next question.

If the performance of Praluent and Eylea is far from ideal, then thanks to which Regeneron medications did its revenue and earnings increase?

To answer this question, in the table below, I have noted their sales.

What additional conclusions do I draw from the table?

I'll start by saying that I can call Regeneron's partnership with Sanofi more than successful, even though it was slightly marred by mixed results [the AERIFY-2 study was a failure, while AERIFY-1 was successful] with its anti-IL33 monoclonal antibody, called itepekimab, which I considered a potential successor to Dupixent in the treatment of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

Sales of the superstar Dupixent are approaching the €4 billion mark, reaching around €3.83 billion in Q2 2025, up 16% year-over-year, and beating my expectations by $150 million.

Digging a little deeper, Regeneron's revenue from its collaboration with Sanofi amounted to $1.44 billion, a 22.1% increase compared to Q1 2024.

These are great numbers, but what is driving the rise in demand for Dupixent?

In my opinion, the key reasons are its FDA approvals for the treatment of several IMIDs over the past 12 months, including bullous pemphigoid [June 20, 2025], chronic spontaneous urticaria [April 18, 2025], chronic obstructive pulmonary disease [September 27, 2024], and chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps [September 13, 2024].

Also, I'll just mention that, according to Regeneron's Form 10-Q, its 'development balance' reached $1.2 billion, a decrease of about $253 million quarter-on-quarter.

I discussed the meaning and importance of the 'development balance', or "contingent reimbursement obligation to Sanofi", in a previous article.

I'll continue by discussing its flagship in the oncology franchise, called Libtayo.

As you know, it is an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody, whose direct competitors are Bristol-Myers Squibb's Opdivo/Opdivo Qvantig (BMY), Merck's Keytruda (MRK), Roche Holding's Tecentriq, and AstraZeneca's Imfinzi (AZN).

However, despite this competition, its total sales reached $377 million for the 3 months ended June 30, 2025, an increase of 26.9% year-on-year.

Among the most recent milestones, I would like to highlight the results of the Phase 3 EMPOWER-Lung 3 study, published on September 9, which has been ongoing for more than 5 years.

In this trial [NCT03409614], Libtayo [cemiplimab] plus chemotherapy significantly outperformed chemotherapy alone in the treatment of patients suffering from NSCLC [metastatic non-small cell lung cancer].

More specifically, the mPFS [median progression-free survival] was 8.2 months compared to 5.5 months in the chemotherapy group [HR=0.58], and the objective response rate, to my surprise, was almost 2 times higher [43.6% vs. 22.1%].

Also, those patients who received the combination of Regeneron's anti-PD-1 therapy with chemotherapy had a median overall survival of 21.1 months compared to 12.9% in the comparison group [platinum-doublet chemotherapy].

Risks

Below in the table, I have highlighted the risks associated with Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, ranging from the ongoing pressure on the US pharmaceutical industry caused by statements by Trump and Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to the decline in demand for Eylea for the reasons I discussed earlier.

Takeaway

Regeneron, like most Big Pharma players, is not yet favored by Mr. Market, for reasons I discussed earlier in the article.

These include the fact that Eylea sales, although beginning to stabilize [$754 million in Q2 2025 vs. $736 million in Q1 2025], are still under pressure from its biosimilars, the 100% tariffs on imports of branded/patented medications announced by Trump on September 25, and the mixed results of itepekimab in the treatment of COPD.

But overall, I believe that this move by the White House will not cause significant damage to Regeneron's gross profit and EBIT margins.

Why do I think so?

Regeneron has a very strong presence in the US, including through the ongoing construction of a new manufacturing facility in Rensselaer, New York, and the agreement signed with Fujifilm on April 22, which, in my opinion, will allow it to significantly increase and partially transfer production of biologics [Dupixent, Libtayo, nezastomig, Eylea HD, and Ordspono] from its "troubled partner" Catalent.

If I look at Regeneron from the other side, it again beat my and Wall Street's EPS expectations by a wide margin, mainly due to surging sales of Libtayo and its IL-4/IL-13 receptor blocker called Dupixent, thereby creating a divergence with its share price, which has been fluctuating in the accumulation zone [from $550 to $600] over the past 6 weeks.

Finally, I want to mention another reason I continue to cover Regeneron with a 'Strong Buy' rating. Its non-GAAP P/E ratio will decline from 14.64x in 2025 to 9.73x in 2029, which is 18.7% and 46% lower than the sector median, respectively.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why I Don't Buy Mortgage REITs
data.description:
If you have read my work here on Seeking Alpha, you will know that I am very bullish on REITs (VNQ).

These vehicles combine the best of both worlds, real estate and stocks, in a tax-efficient vehicle and have, over the long run, beaten most other asset classes, including even the S&P 500 (SPY):

Moreover, REITs are today historically cheap after suffering a 3-year-long bear market, and I think that the coming cuts to interest rates will be a strong catalyst for them.

But that does not mean that I am buying just any REIT. This is a vast sector, and there are major segments that are very undesirable in my opinion.

One that I dislike the most is mortgage REITs, or mREITs in short (MORT). These REITs do not typically own properties. Instead, they will lend money to other real estate investors, taking a similar role as a bank but often focusing on riskier loans.

They are very popular among new REIT investors because they commonly offer much higher dividend yields than traditional equity REITs.

It is not uncommon to find mREITs yielding 10% or more. Some of the biggest ones, like Annaly Capital Management (NLY) and AGNC Investment (AGNC), are yielding as much as 15% right now.

But despite that, I am staying away from them. Here are 5 reasons why:

Reason #1: Poor Historic Track Record

Over the past 20-year time period ending in 2023, mortgage REITs earned only 2% average annual total returns. In comparison, traditional equity REITs earned nearly 5x higher returns:

This serves as a good reminder that earning a high dividend yield is pointless if it leads to significant value destruction over time.

In many cases, the high dividend yield is just a mirage. There is no point in earning a 10% yield if the share price then declines by 10% as well.

Reason #2: Unpredictable Business Model

One of the main reasons why mREITs have done so poorly over time is that their business models are highly dependent on macro factors that are out of their control.

They are commonly highly leveraged and only earn a small spread by borrowing at one rate and lending at another. As a result, even small changes to interest rates or spreads can make or break their businesses. Look at this volatility!

If the REITs could predict these future macro changes, they could adapt their businesses and earn great profits. But history has shown that these macro factors are highly unpredictable, and this explains why so many mREITs have done poorly over time. They have failed to predict major turns in the macro environment time and time again.

Reason #3: External Management

Another issue with many mREITs is that they are externally managed, which often leads to greater conflicts of interest.

Unlike most traditional REITs, which are internally managed and hire their own management, mREITs will commonly outsource the management to an outside company that will earn fees for its management services.

The problem here is that these fees are typically tied to the volume of assets under management, which will then incentivise the manager to grow the REIT’s portfolio as much as possible to earn higher fees.

This then often leads to dilutive equity raises and overleverage, which can be very detrimental to shareholder value.

Reason #4: Unsustainable Dividends

Because of the issues highlighted earlier, the dividend payments of mREITs are also often not sustainable.

Take the example of Annaly Capital Management (NLY), which is one of the leading mREITs. It has repeatedly cut its dividend throughout its history:

This is also a good reminder that a 10% yield can quickly turn to a much lower yield in the mREIT sector.

Traditional equity REITs (VNQ) may not yield as much, but they steadily grow their dividend payments, which add up over time. Take the example of Realty Income (O). It has hiked its dividend for 30 years in a row. Not even the great financial crisis or the pandemic could stop its dividend growth:

It is much safer and more rewarding to earn a 5% yield growing at 5% per year than buying a flat and a likely unsustainable 10% yield.

Reason #5: Questionable Risk-to-Reward

Taking all of this into account, I find the risk-to-reward of these vehicles unappealing.

Yes, the yield is enticing, but it often isn’t sustainable and will be matched with value destruction over time.

If you think that you can predict major turns in macro factors like interest rates, then mREIT may be an attractive trading vehicle, but as a long-term-oriented investor, I don’t see the point in most of them.

As always, some exceptions exist, of course, but generally speaking, I would much rather invest in traditional equity REITs that own properties.

Their businesses are more resilient, and the management is typically better aligned with shareholders.

Opportunities are abundant today with many high-quality REITs trading at exceptionally low valuations, and therefore, we just don’t see the point in speculating with mREITs at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: TIM S.A.: From Mobile Underdog In Brazil To Cash-Flow Champion
data.description:
The recent figures posted by TIM S.A. (NYSE:TIMB) over 1H25 have confirmed what the market has been pricing in: TIM delivering the most solid results in the telecom sector in Brazil.

Despite still being number three in the sector, TIM maintains its ARPU leadership, has increasingly brought stronger cash flow generation, as well as margin expansion and revenue growth on all fronts—with a strong emphasis on post-paid and mobile services.

Even with the advance in 5G coverage in Brazil, leaner CapEx has translated into greater efficiency for TIM, and consequently higher returns and growing shareholder distribution potential.

TIMB's thesis still represents a stable, cash-flow-driven opportunity attractive to income seeker-investors, sustained by top-line growth above inflation, generous payouts, and a misalignment of multiples in terms of cash generation versus peers, not consistent with TIM's already prolonged superiority in covering CapEx with cash flows.

A Rapid Recap

In February of that year, in my latest coverage of TIMB, I wrote:

“TIM S.A. reported a solid and unsurprising Q4, although the bottom line was slightly disappointing, mainly due to issues with the services mix. Most importantly, however, was the robust guidance for 2025 and 2027, reinforcing appealing dividend distributions potentially yielding around 8-9% over the next two years, which are still attractive given Brazil's equity risk premium of 7.6%. Revisiting the valuation model, with TIMB able to distribute $1.35 in annual dividends, I updated my share price target to $16.83 for the TIM S.A. ADR.”

As it turns out, my projections turned out to be overly cautious, with TIMB hitting $21 per share today, marking a rise of more than 80% over the course of the year.

And the big reason for this re-rating in TIMB is primarily due to an upward revision in results, with the results posted mainly in 1Q25 and 2Q25 being the strongest in the telecom sector in Brazil. In the last six months, analysts have expected revenues for 2025, 2026, and 2027 to be around 8% lower than what they project today.

But TIMB's strong performance is also derived from a more favorable macro environment for Brazilian equities in general, based on (1) the prospect that the monetary tightening policy will have reached its peak with the Selic rate at 15%; and (2) a more appreciated BRL throughout this year, implying greater visibility of inflation control moving forward.

The more specific drivers that have highlighted TIM's bull run this year have been driven by (1) an acceleration in mobile/paid revenue growth with a growing ARPU; (2) a high scale of 5G migration increasing the company's efficiency; (3) a very strict cost discipline enabling an expansion of margins; and all of this leading to what I think is the most important point for the defensive-income thesis being (4) strong cash flow generations supporting dividends and new growth verticals beyond core mobile.

Inside TIM’s Numbers

To go deeper into TIM's recent developments, let's divide the main points into five blocks.

1. Mobile-led Service Revenue Growth:

In 2Q25, TIM saw its mobile services grow faster than its total services (5.6% YoY vs. 5.1% YoY), demonstrating that the core business has been expanding not only at the industry average, but also above inflation, which currently stands at 5.1% per year. In addition, TIM posted the highest mobile ARPU in Brazil (~33 BRL) surpassing Telefônica Brasil (VIV) which posted (~31 BRL), expanding in mid-single digits as a consequence of tailwinds from a more rational competitive scenario in Brazil in the face of the incorporation of part of Oi's (OTC:OIBZQ) mobile assets by the top three telecoms (Vivo, TIM and Claro) leaving the market less fragmented and reducing the need for aggressive promotions.

But another point I'd like to highlight is the latent penetration of postpaid within mobile services revenue. In 2Q25, TIM managed to add more than 450,000 customers. That's fourteen consecutive quarters of double-digit postpaid revenue growth (+12.2% YoY in 1H25). And this is exceptional because it creates a mix toward higher-value customers who generate recurring revenue with low churn.

This is where I want to open a parenthesis about the Brazilian telephony culture.

In Brazil, telecoms have, over the years, intentionally sought to “squeeze” the prepaid customer, using pricing and bundles to “push” the customer as far as possible towards control/post, which is precisely where the revenue growth is.

For example, TIM's "control" postpaid plan with 33GB of mobile internet starts at 44 BRL. Although a prepaid plan can be more economical, for customers who need internet all month long on a consistent basis, the prepaid packages end up being more expensive, reaching BRL 50 to BRL 60 per month, with smaller franchises than the 33GB of control. What's more, there are plans such as TIM's "Pre XIP Plus" which require a minimum top-up of up to BRL 30 in order to enjoy the benefits of data-free social networking, making it highly unfavorable to choose prepaid.

In short, in Brazil, postpaid is no longer seen as an "expensive luxury", while in countries such as the US, Europe, India, etc., prepaid is still very strong based on competition from very cheap MVNOs, lower loyalty charges, and a greater habit of controlling spending.

2. 5G rollout, Traffic migration, and Operational Efficiency:

The second point I want to highlight is that TIM's 5G now represents 30% of total traffic-about 36% concentrated in Sao Paulo), and is number one in coverage in the country, which has also helped to reduce the cost of GB by 30% of 4G.

The consequence at the end is a gain in efficiency. Offloading traffic improves network utilization and lowers unit costs, which ultimately helps to increase margins. For example, in 2Q25, adjusted EBITDA margins stood at 50.8%, an increase of 0.8 p.p. YoY, and a sequential increase of 2.5 p.p.

This margin expansion is also a reflection of TIM's "efficiency program", where it seeks to maintain cost growth below inflation (currently at 5.1% annualized) by covering all expense lines—lease renegotiations, tower sharing, etc.

3. Cash Flow Growth and Shareholder Returns Outlook:

Bottom-line synergies lead to more robust cash generation. In the first half of 2025, operating free cash flow expanded by 177% year-over-year, standing at R$1.42 billion, versus H1 2024. This implies around 22% of adjusted EBITDA generated in the period being converted into FCF, which is in line with industry norms for growth/CapEx.

TIM's guidance has been the same since February of this year, estimating OpCF (EBITDA-after leases minus CapEx) growth until 2027 at a CAGR of between 11%-14%, which would imply OpCF in 2027 of between R$6.3 billion and R$6.8 billion—considering that in the last twelve months OpFCF has been at R$5.5 billion. With regard to shareholder remuneration, TIM's objective remains to deliver between R$4.5 billion and R$4.67 billion per year between 2025 and 2027.

If TIM's market value in BRL is considered, at R$55.29 billion, this would put an estimated dividend yield of ~8.1% to ~8.5% per year, considering only dividends. However, the consensus points to a potential EPS CAGR of 7.3% between 2025 and 2029, which could potentially suggest that, in addition to the current attractive yield, the stock offers potential for capital appreciation over the next few years.

On Valuations

Updating the Gordon Growth Model for TIMB, using as a base the projected EPS for 2027 (not a spike year like 2025) of $1.73 vs. $1.49 in 2025E, and keeping the same payout (100% for simplicity), long-term growth rates of 3%, and a cost of equity (Ke) of 10. 5% (from CAPM: Rf = 7.9%, β = 0.5, ERP = 7.67%), this would reflect a price target of $23.76-an upside of 11.6% vs. the latest share price of $21.3.

It's important to note that over the past four years, TIM's payout has exceeded 100% every time (even reaching 200% last year).

Furthermore, compared to its other two main peers, Telefônica Brasil (Vivo) and América Móvil (AMX) (Claro), despite trading at very close multiples (mainly EV/EBITDA), TIM trades at a somewhat relevant discount in terms of earnings and free cash flow.

And here, I'd also like to go into a risk analysis of TIM's bullish thesis.

I wouldn't say that the discount on TIMB's EV/EBITDA and P/E multiples is necessarily unjustified, after all, both Vivo and Claro have more diversified operations in fixed services (pay-TV and integrated corporate) while TIM is more dependent on mobile—which actually offers a slightly higher operational risk.

In addition, Claro/América Móvil has a continental presence in LatAm, diluting local risks, and Vivo is the market leader in Brazil in terms of recent share and post-paid customers, giving it greater pricing power.

TIM, meanwhile, grew a lot after the incorporation of Oi Móvel, but is still seen as the #3 player in Brazil, in theory passing on more limited pricing power— even though it currently has the highest ARPU.

But where I do see an unjustified discount is in relation to cash flows–which is arguably the point that interests me most when looking at telecoms.

As the graph below shows, although in theory TIM should have greater reinvestment pressure relative to the size of the business as it rushes to match network and new services, TIMB actually appears to be structurally more efficient—especially since 2023. The company's cash flow covers CapEx much more easily than Vivo and Claro, suggesting a greater capacity to generate FCF, and in theory, sustain dividends.

Therefore, even with this higher valuation indicator, TIMB trades at valuation multiples equal to or below VIV and AMX, so I would say that the discount here seems unwarranted, which also helps to support the thesis that there is room for multiple expansion for TIMB compared to its main peers.

Final Remarks

TIM S.A. is enjoying excellent operational momentum, as is the telecom sector in Brazil, but also on its own merits, highlighted by its excellent performance in monetizing its mobile base and transitioning it to post-paid.

Continued cost discipline and lower CapEx investments open up more room for free cash flow numbers and can make short and long-term guidance look conservative—especially in terms of shareholder returns.

Following in the footsteps of the more optimistic top and bottom line market consensus, I am bullish on TIMB, with a target of $23.76, believing it to be a comfortable ceiling, especially considering a dividend yield of ~8.1% to ~8.5% going forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SQQQ: Smart Play For Protecting The Technology Focused Investment
data.description:
Let us face it, the US equity market is currently all about technology growth. Like many investors, my investment portfolio has a significant technology exposure, including growth income positions like option income funds, etc, which have a lot of technology exposure in the underlying portfolios. I have been actively working on the hedging solutions as a growth protection. ProShares UltraPro Short QQQ ETF (NASDAQ:SQQQ) frequently pops up as an effective hedging approach for my short-term protection cycles.

My last coverage of SQQQ in January was based on my thinking that SQQQ should be an interesting tool for the seemly volatile US equity market in 2025. The hedging strategy has been working well for me. As a new update, I will continue to rate SQQQ as HOLD. I plan to keep some SQQQ shares and play some SQQQ options to protect my growth portfolio, despite the "short" nature of the ETF.

SQQQ ETF Update

SQQQ is a leveraged ETF from ProShares with a focus on the ultra inverse effects on the Nasdaq-100 Index. It is designed to triple the inverse (-3x) exposure to the daily price move of the Nasdaq-100 Index.

The ETF strategy has not been changed much since my last coverage. Readers can refer to my previous write-up for some of the fund details. Below, I'll add some holding-specific information so one can have a feel for what exactly is included in the fund.

As one can see from the above data, SQQQ owns many swap products to construct its ultra-short positions against the NASDAQ 100 Index. These swap deals are from different financial institutions/banks for perhaps diversification purposes.

The underlying portfolio for the (inverse) leverage is well represented by the NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQ), which has become increasingly popular in recent years. In fact, QQQ ETF has seen significant growth in market capitalization, reaching over $380 billion at the moment. Its trading volume is also reaching an astonishing level of about 54 million per day. All of these have been driven by investor confidence and huge interest in the explosive technology growth in the US. The technology sector and its core holdings, including the "Magnificent Seven" companies, have been doing very well in the equity market for the last 20 years or so.

On the other hand, SQQQ has been very active in the market as well, with its daily trading volume over 100 million. This is remarkable considering the fact that the ETF has a high expense ratio of 0.95%. The lesser-known number is the SQQQ option volume, which is measured at around the 201k level recently. The option activities are also good evidence showing a high interest in playing the mega trend of technology growth.

Risk Management is Very Critical at the Current Conjunction

Since 2025, the US equity market has been getting very crowded in AI technology plays. We have seen that all the trillion-dollar market-cap companies (listed in the table below, excluding Berkshire Hathaway) have been labeled AI names. As a result, the high concentration of S&P has reached 37% for the top 9 holdings as shown in the portfolio of SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY).

Keep in mind that the technology concentration in the S&P 500 is a widely watched indicator for investors who may be highly worried about market bubbles and the possibility of a big market correction that could occur soon.

I think SQQQ can be a good hedging tool for to consider for investors with heavy technology exposures, such as QQQ and SPY. For long-term investors, the hedging method is rather a protection measure that may be done on a short-term basis, such as when the technology allocation reaches a certain extreme level.

Watch out for the Market Seasonality

We are in a historically weak season of the year for technology. The following data shows some seasonal patterns for QQQ based on the monthly price performance. Notice that September appears to be the slowest month based on the 10-year data, and December is the other month showing a loss.

Keep in mind that the data is based on past years, and there is no guarantee that similar seasonality patterns would occur for the present year or future months. However, I use the data as a guide to enable the SQQQ positions. For example, in any month with less than 1.5% gain in the past 10 years, there will be some SQQQ hedging position created. This would include February, April, August, September, and December. The approach will be more efficient than holding the "short" hedging for the whole year.

Notice that the coming (Q3) earnings season may come in at a time when the market is hovering at the recent record level and the S&P 500 valuation is around the historical high. The market expectation for the upcoming earnings is also very high. I believe that this appears to be a very unfavorable market setup and that we might see a big market pullback when there is a moderate earnings disappointment. The wild price changes during the past earnings seasons observed in recent trading have already indicated that there is a substantial nervousness among the investors towards the growth plays.

I have increased my SQQQ positions for the coming months for my growth protection. I am also looking into the SQQQ option strategies to identify cost-effective and time-efficient solutions (See more details in the next section).

It is more Cost-Efficient to Hedge against Option Income Plays

Many investors have invested in some high-yield option income ETFs with QQQ as the underlying portfolio. I have been building significant positions with the names I covered in the past, including NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) and Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ).

Based on my study, I believe that the SQQQ inverse effect could be up to 4 times the daily price move of the QQQI or GPIQ. This is more than (-1) 3x on the QQQ itself. Such price patterns have been observed during a period of a few days and are highly subject to the volatility level. I typically use the effect multiples as a reference to determine the size of my SQQQ position to protect my growth income portfolio. One simple case is to allocate 1/4 of the portfolio's worth for hedging the portfolio with QQQI as the major holding. The hedging gain may offset some price loss during a downturn period. Such a gain could be used to add more QQQI shares late and recover some income distribution accordingly.

My favorite approach is to sell SQQQ PUT options to start the protection cycle. There are many benefits involved with it. The first is that the weekly options can be relatively simple to manage. In fact, I could keep the weekly option rolling for the entire protection process. The stoppage of the protection can be as simple as just not selling the new PUT for the new week.

The second aspect is that the weekly time decay is on the seller's side for the SQQQ Put options, which could expire worthless more often compared to the longer duration options, and the whole premium is kept as profit (income). This would improve the overall hedging cost greatly during the times when the market could be moderately volatile. Please see my previous write-up on SQQQ for more details about the mentioned option trading.

Last but not least takeaway is that my regular option activities, including weekly PUT writing, offer unique insights for my market research and can help manage volatility more effectively. However, it is worth noting that the option strategy should be primarily used by experienced traders, not beginners.

Risk and Caveat

The ultra-leveraged ETF, like SQQQ, could elevate the volatility to an extreme level, such that the position could lose value very quickly. This could happen when the market moves against the investor's projection. For example, a QQQ bull run will destroy SQQQ value in a hurry. For many investors, any SQQQ positions should be considered as a "cost" for valuation purposes.

Before investing and trading SQQQ, investors need to read carefully the official risk warnings documented on the SQQQ ETF site and FINRA's regulatory reminder.

As mentioned earlier, the option strategy discussed in this write-up is for option-savvy investors. The PUT-writing may often be considered a risk-confined strategy. But keep in mind, the options for ultra-leverage ETFs are extremely time sensitive, so they are not really suitable for passive investors.

Conclusion

The US stock market appears to be overextended as a whole. The market overvaluation makes the growth plays like technology-heavy QQQ increasingly vulnerable. I would expect market corrections to occur in the near future. SQQQ, as a 3-times inverse leveraged ETF for the NASDAQ 100 Index, could serve as a short-term hedging solution. The actively traded options could be looked at as a more efficient approach in cycle management and possibly reducing the hedging cost.
